text:
HEMATOLOGY/MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT -- METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Routine follow-up for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation. Patient presents for scheduled oncology assessment at approximately 9 months on cabozantinib and 18 months from initial diagnosis. Restaging CT scheduled to be reviewed today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with a well-established history of Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (cT3bN1M1, WHO/ISUP grade 4 with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, IMDC intermediate risk) who presents for his routine oncology follow-up. His disease course has been complex and is summarized below.

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago after presenting with a three-week history of gross hematuria, right flank pain, and significant unintentional weight loss (approximately 15 lbs over three months). Staging workup confirmed an 11.4 cm right renal mass with extension into the right renal vein and bilateral pulmonary metastases, as well as retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) identified VHL frameshift deletion, PBRM1 and BAP1 loss-of-function mutations, SETD2 loss, and notably MET amplification (copy number 6.8) along with CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion and TP53 R248W. PD-L1 by 22C3 CPS was 18.

After multidisciplinary tumor board review, he underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 16 months ago. Final surgical pathology confirmed clear cell RCC with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive tumor necrosis (>50%), lymphovascular invasion, renal vein thrombus, and 3/8 retroperitoneal lymph nodes positive. Negative surgical margins (R0). PD-L1 CPS was 22 in the sarcomatoid areas.

Approximately 4 weeks post-nephrectomy, he initiated first-line systemic therapy with ipi 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by transition to Opdivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. He tolerated the induction regimen reasonably well; his principal immunotherapy-related toxicity was grade 2 hypothyroidism developing after cycle 2, managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, which has remained a chronic medication requirement.

First restaging imaging after completing the 4 induction cycles demonstrated a mixed response: pulmonary nodules were shrinking (right lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, right upper lobe nodule from 0.6 to 0.4 cm), while new retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal deposits were identified. The aortocaval region showed a 2.1 cm new conglomerate, and a new 1.6 cm right common iliac nodal mass had appeared. This discordant pattern raised initial consideration of pseudoprogression, but on two subsequent scans over the next three months, nodal disease continued to grow (right common iliac node enlarging to 3.1 cm, aortocaval conglomerate to 3.4 cm, new right external iliac node) while pulmonary lesions remained stable. After discussion at multidisciplinary review, this was attributed to differential biology between the responding conventional clear cell component (pulmonary sites) and a potentially immunotherapy-resistant sarcomatoid-predominant subclone driving nodal progression. Opdivo was discontinued at the 9-month mark from initial systemic therapy, and the patient was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib given the MET amplification on NGS and its VEGFR/MET/AXL inhibitory profile.

Cabozantinib was initiated at 60 mg orally once daily. In the first several weeks, he developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (bilateral palmar and plantar erythema with blistering) and grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 stools above baseline), necessitating a dose reduction to 40 mg orally once daily. He also developed grade 2 hypertension requiring addition of amlodipine 5 mg, subsequently uptitrated to 10 mg, to his existing lisinopril 20 mg. These toxicities stabilized over the ensuing weeks: HFS improved to grade 1, diarrhea to grade 1, and blood pressure came under better control.

First restaging on cabozantinib at approximately 3 months showed an encouraging partial response in the nodal compartment: right common iliac nodal mass shrank from 3.1 cm to 2.3 cm, aortocaval conglomerate from 3.4 cm to 2.6 cm, obturator node from 1.8 to 1.4 cm, and right external iliac node from 1.2 to 0.9 cm. Pulmonary lesions remained stable. No new lesions. He continued cabozantinib 40 mg daily without dose re-escalation.

Second restaging at 6 months on cabozantinib confirmed stable disease, with all nodal and pulmonary targets essentially unchanged from the 3-month assessment. Of note, a new 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in the right iliac bone was identified on CT, but a confirmatory whole-body technetium bone scan showed no convincing evidence of osseous metastatic disease; the focus was attributed to a reactive/degenerative etiology. The patient was reassured.

Today he presents at the 9-month mark on cabozantinib (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis) for his scheduled follow-up visit. He reports he has been taking cabozantinib 40 mg daily with good adherence -- he mentions he takes it in the evening with a small snack to help reduce nausea. He endorses ongoing low-grade chronic side effects that he has learned to manage: his hands and feet continue to require daily application of urea cream and careful moisturizing, and he has adapted his footwear. He notes intermittent loose stools (1-2 above his baseline per day on most days) that he manages with dietary adjustments and occasional loperamide as needed. He reports some persistent dysgeusia -- food tastes "off" or metallic, particularly with red meat and sweets -- which has been present for several months and affects his appetite. Despite this, his weight has been essentially stable at approximately 178 lbs (down from 198 lbs at diagnosis, most of which was lost prior to treatment). He denies any new symptoms of dyspnea, hemoptysis, or chest pain that would suggest pulmonary progression. He has no new bone pain. No headaches or neurologic symptoms. He reports his energy level is fair -- he is able to participate in light activity and has returned to some part-time consulting work from home, which he finds meaningful. He denies significant depression, although he acknowledges the psychological burden of living with metastatic disease and has been engaged with survivorship counseling and the cancer support group at our institution.

Blood pressure at home has been running in the 130s-140s systolic per his log, which he brings to the visit.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Weight stable at approximately 178 lbs; ongoing mild fatigue, grade 1; appetite mildly reduced secondary to dysgeusia; no fever, chills, or night sweats.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension, managed on dual antihypertensive therapy; no chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema (resolved since prior TKI toxicity management).
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, hemoptysis, or cough. No pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Intermittent loose stools (grade 1 diarrhea), 1-2 above baseline per day; manageable. No frank hematochezia, melena, or significant abdominal pain. Mild dysgeusia affecting food enjoyment. No nausea at current dose.
Genitourinary: No hematuria since nephrectomy (approximately 16 months ago). Mildly reduced urinary stream baseline from prior nephrectomy; creatinine stable on recent labs.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain. Mild plantar tenderness related to chronic HFS.
Skin/integument: Chronic grade 1 hand-foot skin reaction, bilateral hands and feet; managed with topical therapy and footwear modifications. No new rash.
Neurologic: Dysgeusia grade 1; no headaches, focal neurologic deficits, or cognitive changes.
Endocrine: History of grade 2 immunotherapy-related hypothyroidism; currently well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Underlying type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin; blood sugars stable per patient report.
Hematologic/lymphatic: No bleeding, bruising, or new lymphadenopathy appreciated by patient.
Psychiatric: Coping with metastatic diagnosis; engaged with survivorship counseling; mood reported as fair.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/84 mmHg (left arm, sitting); heart rate 72 bpm; respiratory rate 16; temperature 98.4°F; oxygen saturation 97% on room air; weight 178.5 lbs (81.0 kg); height 5'10" (177.8 cm); BMI 25.5.

General: Pleasant, well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Well-nourished, mildly fatigued-appearing but engaged and conversational.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No oral mucositis. Mild dry mouth. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular chains.

Neck: No thyromegaly. No jugular venous distension.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheeze, or diminished breath sounds. Comfortable respiratory effort.

Abdomen: Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed right-sided flank/laparotomy scar from prior nephrectomy. No palpable organomegaly. No palpable abdominal or pelvic masses. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

Musculoskeletal: No tenderness on palpation of axial skeleton or long bones. No point tenderness over right iliac crest.

Skin: Both palms and plantar surfaces of feet show mild erythema and subtle hyperkeratosis, consistent with grade 1 HFS; no active blistering, ulceration, or open skin breakdown. Skin otherwise intact.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x 3. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Cranial nerves grossly intact.

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy on exam today.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS:
Complete Blood Count (recent):
- WBC: 6.4 x10^9/L (within normal limits)
- Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable; consistent with anemia of chronic disease and prior nephrectomy)
- Platelets: 218,000/uL (normalized from thrombocytosis at diagnosis)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L; Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L; Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 22 mg/dL; Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR ~42 mL/min/1.73m2, stable chronic kidney disease stage 3a, as expected in solitary kidney context)
- Glucose: 108 mg/dL (fasting; within acceptable range on metformin)
- AST: 26 U/L; ALT: 29 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase: 78 U/L; Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL (all within normal limits -- no hepatotoxicity)
- Total protein: 7.1 g/dL; Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
- Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL (normal; prior hypercalcemia at diagnosis has resolved)
- LDH: 198 U/L (down significantly from 287 U/L at diagnosis)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily)

Urinalysis: No proteinuria (urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.12; acceptable, monitoring given TKI therapy and hypertension). No hematuria on dipstick.

Lipids/metabolic: No new abnormalities flagged; triglycerides mildly elevated at 188 mg/dL, consistent with TKI effect.

PT/INR: 1.1 (normal).

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast was performed recently (results reviewed in clinic today):

Chest: The right lower lobe pulmonary nodule is stable at approximately 0.7 cm. The left lower lobe nodule remains stable at approximately 0.7 cm. The right upper lobe nodule is stable, unchanged at 0.3 cm. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusions. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen/Pelvis: The previously noted right common iliac nodal mass continues to show a stable appearance, measuring approximately 2.2 cm and without evidence of further interval growth or regression. The aortocaval nodal conglomerate remains at approximately 2.5 cm, stable. The right obturator node is stable at approximately 1.3 cm. The right external iliac node is stable at approximately 0.8 cm. The previously identified right iliac bone sclerotic focus is unchanged, without aggressive features, consistent with the benign/degenerative interpretation on prior bone scan. No new nodal deposits are identified in the pelvis or retroperitoneum. No hepatic lesions. No new osseous lesions.

Overall impression: Stable disease across all measured nodal and pulmonary targets compared to the 6-month restaging scan. No new sites of disease. This represents continuing stability on cabozantinib 40 mg daily, now at approximately the 9-month mark of TKI therapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with Stage IV clear cell RCC (grade 4, 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, IMDC intermediate risk) who is currently on second-line cabozantinib 40 mg daily following progression through first-line ipi/nivo. He presents today with stable disease on restaging CT -- now his best sustained response on this regimen, maintaining disease stability for approximately 6 months (since his partial response was documented at the 3-month mark on cabozantinib). He is approximately 18 months from his original diagnosis.

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RCC -- STABLE DISEASE ON CABOZANTINIB:
Restaging CT today demonstrates sustained stability in all previously identified nodal deposits (right common iliac, aortocaval, right obturator, right external iliac) and in the bilateral pulmonary nodules. No new lesions. This is an encouraging and clinically meaningful result. We discussed this with the patient, who was visibly relieved. The absence of new sites is particularly reassuring given the sarcomatoid differentiation and aggressive genomic profile (BAP1 loss, CDKN2A/B deletion, TP53 mutation, MET amplification). The ongoing disease control is likely attributable in part to MET amplification-driven sensitivity to cabozantinib, as previously hypothesized.

Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose re-escalation or change indicated at this time -- adequate disease control is being achieved, and the patient has acceptable tolerability at 40 mg. Next restaging CT in approximately 3 months. Labs to be repeated at 6-week interval (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, urine protein:creatinine ratio).

2. CABOZANTINIB TOXICITY MANAGEMENT:
a. Hand-Foot Syndrome (grade 1, chronic): Stable. Patient managing well with urea 20% cream twice daily, comfortable footwear, and avoidance of prolonged standing/walking. Reinforced skin care instructions. Counseled to report any return of blistering or grade 2 symptoms promptly.
b. Diarrhea (grade 1, intermittent): Patient managing with low-residue diet and PRN loperamide. No signs of dehydration or electrolyte disturbance (potassium and bicarbonate normal today). Encouraged to maintain good oral hydration. If worsens to grade 2 or beyond, consider formal dose hold and reassessment.
c. Hypertension (grade 2, previously; currently well-controlled): BP 138/84 today. Continue lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. No changes to antihypertensive regimen today. Continue home BP monitoring with log; target <140/90 mmHg. Note: metformin use is acceptable given creatinine 1.8 and eGFR ~42; no change needed. Will continue to monitor renal function closely.
d. Dysgeusia (grade 1): Persistent but not causing significant nutritional compromise; weight stable. Discussed dietary strategies (marinating meats, trying varied flavors, zinc supplementation consideration -- discussed with patient, he is interested, referred to dietitian). No medication change for this.
e. Fatigue (grade 1): Encouraged light aerobic activity as tolerated, energy conservation strategies. Hemoglobin stable; no indication for erythropoiesis-stimulating agent at this time.

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM (IMMUNE-RELATED ENDOCRINOPATHY, CHRONIC):
TSH 2.8 mIU/L, well within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No dose change. Continue current dose indefinitely. Annual endocrinology input will be coordinated as needed.

4. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
Fasting glucose 108 mg/dL; stable on metformin. No dose change needed. HbA1c to be rechecked at next visit. Kidney function stable at eGFR ~42; metformin is at acceptable threshold, will continue to monitor. Patient instructed to report any hypoglycemic episodes.

5. HYPERTENSION (COMORBID + TKI-EXACERBATED):
Well-controlled as noted above. Continue dual antihypertensive regimen.

6. CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE:
Serum creatinine stable at 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR ~42), as expected following right radical nephrectomy with a single functioning kidney. Urine protein:creatinine ratio 0.12, acceptable. No additional intervention needed at this time. Will continue to monitor at each visit and avoid nephrotoxic medications.

7. ANEMIA:
Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL; mild, stable, likely anemia of chronic disease. No active bleeding. No transfusion required. Monitor at regular intervals.

8. QUALITY OF LIFE / PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Patient continues to engage with survivorship counseling and the cancer support group. His return to part-time consulting work is a positive indicator of functional recovery. Reinforced the importance of maintaining these supports during ongoing cancer treatment. Referral to dietitian placed today to address dysgeusia-related appetite changes and optimize nutritional status. Social work check-in offered; patient declined for now but is aware of availability.

9. PLAN SUMMARY:
- Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily (no changes)
- Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily
- Continue lisinopril 20 mg orally once daily
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg orally once daily
- Continue metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
- PRN loperamide for diarrhea as needed
- Continue topical urea 20% cream to hands and feet twice daily
- Labs in 6 weeks: CBC, CMP including LFTs, TSH, urine protein:creatinine ratio, fasting lipids, HbA1c
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months
- Dietitian referral placed today
- Return to clinic in 6-8 weeks for labs and toxicity check; earlier if any new symptoms, grade 2 or higher toxicity, or acute concerns

Time spent with patient: approximately 45 minutes. More than 50% of visit time devoted to discussion of disease status, treatment options, and counseling.

Documented by: Attending Medical Oncologist
Fellow/Resident co-signature: [on file]
